messages.content:
You are an expert at named entity recognition. Given an input text, find all named entities.

The possible labels for named entities are:

* **PER**: names of people (different languages, nicknames, usernames, fictional characters, titles with names, etc.)
* **LOC**: names of cities, countries, landmarks, geographical features, addresses, etc.
* **ORG**: names of companies, institutions, agencies, teams, etc.
* **MISC**: everything else that does not fit into the previous categories, but can still be considered a named entity (events, works of art, nationalities, religions, etc.)

### Tokenization rules

* Split the input text into tokens by **whitespace** only.
* Each token is counted as one unit (words, punctuation marks, numbers, symbols all count as tokens).
* Tokens are indexed starting from 0.

### Output format

Return the result as a **list of JSON dictionaries** in this exact format:

```json
[
  {
    "entity_group": "<ENTITY_TYPE>",
    "word": "<ENTITY_TEXT>",
    "token_start": <TOKEN_START_INDEX>,
    "token_end": <TOKEN_END_INDEX>
  },
  ...
]
```

### Rules

* `entity_group` must be one of the specified labels.
* `word` must exactly match the original text substring, including punctuation if present.
* `token_start` is the index of the first token in the entity (0-based).
* `token_end` is the index of the last token in the entity (end-inclusive).
* Only output the JSON array — no explanations, no extra text.

### Example

**Input:**

```
I'm Radek Stulc, I was born in Prague, and I am currently studying at CTU.
```

**Tokenization:**

```
["I'm", "Radek", "Stulc,", "I", "was", "born", "in", "Prague,", "and", "I", "am", "currently", "studying", "at", "CTU."]
```

**Output:**

```json
[
  {
    "entity_group": "PER",
    "word": "Radek Stulc,",
    "token_start": 1,
    "token_end": 2
  },
  {
    "entity_group": "LOC",
    "word": "Prague,",
    "token_start": 7,
    "token_end": 7
  },
  {
    "entity_group": "ORG",
    "word": "CTU.",
    "token_start": 14,
    "token_end": 14
  }
]
```

Charles IV (German: Karl IV.; Czech: Karel IV.; Latin: Carolus IV; 14 May 1316 – 29 November 1378[1]), also known as Charles of Luxembourg, born Wenceslaus (German: Wenzel, Czech: Václav),[2] was Holy Roman Emperor from 1355 until his death in 1378. He was elected King of Germany (King of the Romans) in 1346 and became King of Bohemia (as Charles I) that same year. He was a member of the House of Luxembourg from his father's side and the Bohemian House of Přemyslid from his mother's side; he emphasized the latter due to his lifelong affinity for the Bohemian side of his inheritance, and also because his direct ancestors in the Přemyslid line included two saints.[3][4]

"Charles IV (German: Karl IV.; Czech: Karel IV.; Latin: Carolus IV; 14 May 1316 – 29 November 1378[1]), also known as Charles of Luxembourg, born Wenceslaus (German: Wenzel, Czech: Václav),[2] was Holy Roman Emperor from 1355 until his death in 1378. He was elected King of Germany (King of the Romans) in 1346 and became King of Bohemia (as Charles I) that same year. He was a member of the House of Luxembourg from his father's side and the Bohemian House of Přemyslid from his mother's side; he emphasized the latter due to his lifelong affinity for the Bohemian side of his inheritance, and also because his direct ancestors in the Přemyslid line included two saints.[3][4]"

Check punctuation tokens: tokens include punctuation attached. Entities may include punctuation.

Now identify entities:

PER: Charles IV, Charles of Luxembourg, Wenceslaus, Charles I. Also maybe "Charles IV" is a person. "Charles I" is a person. "Charles of Luxembourg" is a person. "Wenceslaus" is a person. Also "Charles IV" appears again? Only once. Also "Charles I" appears. Also "Charles IV" at start. Also "Charles" alone? But "Charles" as part of "Charles IV" is part of entity. "Charles" alone maybe not entity.

ORG: Holy Roman Emperor? That's a title, not an organization. But "House of Luxembourg", "House of Přemyslid" are organizations (families). Also "Holy Roman Emperor" maybe MISC? It's a title. "King of Germany" maybe title. "King of Bohemia" title. "King of the Romans" title. "German" "Czech" "Latin" are languages. "German:" etc. "German:" not entity. "Czech:" etc. "Latin:" not entity. "Přemyslid" is a dynasty, maybe MISC? But "House of Přemyslid" is ORG. "House of Luxembourg" ORG. "Holy Roman Emperor" maybe MISC. "King of Germany" MISC. "King of Bohemia" MISC. "King of the Romans" MISC. "Bohemian" maybe MISC. "Přemyslid line" MISC. "Saints" MISC.

LOC: maybe "Germany", "Bohemia", "Luxembourg" is a country. "Luxembourg" is a country. "Germany" is a country. "Bohemia" is a historical region. "Luxembourg" also a city? But it's a country. So LOC. "Luxembourg" appears as part of "Charles of Luxembourg" but that is part of PER. But "Luxembourg" alone appears as part of that. But we might treat "Luxembourg" as part of PER. But also "Luxembourg" as a location. But we should not double tag. We need to decide. Usually "Charles of Luxembourg" is a person, but "Luxembourg" is a location. But the entity is "Charles of Luxembourg" as a person. So we don't tag "Luxembourg" separately. But "Germany" and "Bohemia" are LOC. Also "Luxembourg" as part of ORG? But it's part of PER. So we skip separate.

Also "Holy Roman Emperor" maybe MISC. "King of Germany" MISC. "King of Bohemia" MISC. "King of the Romans" MISC.

Also "Přemyslid" maybe MISC? But "House of Přemyslid" ORG.

Also "Charles IV" is PER. "Charles I" is PER. "Wenceslaus" is PER.

Also "Charles IV" appears with "German: Karl IV.; Czech: Karel IV.; Latin: Carolus IV;" These are alternative names. Should we tag "Karl IV." as PER? It's a variant of the same person. But it's part of the same entity "Charles IV". The tokenization splits "Karl" and "IV.;". So "Karl IV." is separate tokens. But we might treat "Karl IV." as part of the same entity. But the entity "Charles IV" includes the tokens 0-1. But "Karl IV." tokens 4-5. But we might not tag them separately. But maybe we should tag "Karl IV." as PER too. But the guidelines: find all named entities. So we should include all variants. So "Karl IV." is a PER. "Karel IV." is PER. "Carolus IV;" is PER. But "IV;" token includes semicolon. So we need to include punctuation. So we need to include tokens 4-5 as "Karl IV.;" token 4 "Karl" token 5 "IV.;". So entity word "Karl IV.;" token_start 4 token_end 5. Similarly "Karel IV.;" tokens 7-8. "Carolus IV;" tokens 10-11. Also "Charles I" tokens 62-63. Also "Charles IV" tokens 0-1. Also "Charles of Luxembourg" tokens 22-24? Actually tokens 22 "Charles" 23 "of" 24 "Luxembourg,". So entity "Charles of Luxembourg," token_start 22 token_end 24. Also "Wenceslaus" tokens 26-27? Actually token 26 "Wenceslaus" token 27 "("? Wait token 27 is "(" after Wenceslaus. So entity "Wenceslaus" token_start 26 token_end 26. Also "Wenzel," token 29. "Václav)," token 31. So "Wenzel," token_start 29 token_end 29. "Václav)," token_start 31 token_end 31. Also "Charles I" tokens 62-63. Also "Charles IV" tokens 0-1.

Also "House of Luxembourg" tokens 73-75? Actually tokens 73 "House" 74 "of" 75 "Luxembourg". So entity "House of Luxembourg" token_start 73 token_end 75. Also "House of Přemyslid" tokens 82-85? Actually tokens 82 "House" 83 "of" 84 "Přemyslid". So entity "House of Přemyslid" token_start 82 token_end 84.

Also "Holy Roman Emperor" tokens 34-36. So entity "Holy Roman Emperor" token_start 34 token_end 36. Also "King of Germany" tokens 47-49? Actually tokens 47 "King" 48 "of" 49 "Germany". So entity "King of Germany" token_start 47 token_end 49. Also "King of the Romans" tokens 50-53? Actually tokens 50 "(King" 51 "of" 52 "the" 53 "Romans)". But parentheses included. So entity "(King of the Romans)" token_start 50 token_end 53. Also "King of Bohemia" tokens 58-60. So entity "King of Bohemia" token_start 58 token_end 60. Also "Bohemian House of Přemyslid" maybe? But we already have House of Přemyslid. "Bohemian" is adjective. But "Bohemian House of Přemyslid" maybe ORG? But we can skip.

Also "Luxembourg" as part of "Charles of Luxembourg" is part of PER. But "Luxembourg" alone appears as token 24 "Luxembourg,". Already part of PER. So no separate.

"Whiskers the cat was known in the neighborhood for his playful antics, especially his relentless pursuit of fluttering butterflies. Every morning, he would dart across the garden, leaping from flower to flower, chasing the bright wings with a gleam in his eyes. But beneath his carefree exterior, Whiskers carried a secret: he was a covert operative for the feline intelligence agency, Agent Purr. His butterfly chases were actually reconnaissance missions, gathering intel on the local wildlife. One sunny afternoon, a particularly elusive butterfly led him to a hidden garden where a group of rival cats plotted a heist. Using his stealth and agility, Whiskers intercepted the plan, leaving the butterflies bewildered and the rival cats thwarted. After the mission, he returned to his garden, purring contentedly, unaware that his butterfly chases were the key to keeping the neighborhood safe. The next day, he chased another butterfly, blissfully unaware of the covert operation he had just completed."
